steps.title: Block the overall pile (pencil)
steps.description: Make sure you're in pencil mode. Lightly sketch a low mounded shape for the heaped net — wider than tall, with an irregular humped silhouette. Let the top edge bulge unevenly; nets don't pile up symmetrically.

steps.title: Find the draping lines (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, sketch two or three long curves flowing along the top of the pile, following the way the netting drapes. These are the high points of folds in the net.

steps.title: Plan the trailing edges (pencil)
steps.description: Sketch a few loose curves spilling off the sides and front of the pile — the parts of the net that hang loose. One or two can pool onto the dock surface.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the silhouette
steps.description: Switch to ink. Trace the outline of the pile and the trailing edges in one mostly continuous pass. Let your line wobble — a stiff outline kills the soft feel of net.

steps.title: Ink the fold lines (ink)
steps.description: Still in ink, trace the draping curves you sketched across the top. Each one is a single confident stroke that follows the form of the heap.

steps.title: Suggest the mesh (ink)
steps.description: Add a sparse pattern of small diamonds or crosshatch marks in a few small patches across the net — never the whole surface. The viewer fills in the rest of the mesh from these hints.

steps.title: Add the floats and weights (ink)
steps.description: Along one trailing edge, add three or four small circles for cork floats. Along another, add a few short oblong shapes for lead weights. These details tell the viewer it's a working net.

steps.title: Clean up
steps.description: Switch to the eraser and remove any pencil that distracts. Resist adding more mesh — the net reads better with quiet stretches than with every knot drawn.
